ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'tea beverage' class (FOODON_03311737). No specific class exists for iced peach tea with konjac jelly; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 AN000A covers tea and tea-based beverages broadly. No specific code exists for iced peach tea with konjac jelly topping; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 74242007 represents tea as a substance. No specific concept for iced peach tea with konjac jelly; closest_parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard takeaway cup of iced peach tea with konjac jelly, approximately 500 ml, as typically served at Taiwanese-style tea shops in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Sweetened iced tea with fruit flavouring and konjac jelly — estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for brewed tea, fruit-flavoured sweetened tea, and konjac jelly component data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for iced peach tea with konjac jelly as a composite product. Values are estimated by combining: (1) USDA FDC #171917 'Tea, brewed, prepared with tap water' for the tea base; (2) USDA FDC #174856 'Beverages, tea, ready-to-drink, sweetened, lemon-flavoured' as a proxy for sweetened fruit-flavoured iced tea; and (3) published konjac jelly nutritional data (glucomannan-based jelly, approximately 5–8 kcal per 100 g, negligible protein and fat, ~1.5 g dietary fibre per 100 g). The final composite reflects a typical 60:40 ratio of sweetened peach tea to konjac jelly by weight, adjusted to per-100 g basis. Sugar content estimated from typical Taiwanese tea shop formulations (30–50% sugar level). Sodium is low as no salt is added.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; sweetened fruit-flavoured tea beverages used as analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for iced peach tea with konjac jelly. GI estimated at 55 based on: (1) sweetened fruit-flavoured tea beverages in the University of Sydney GI Database typically fall in the GI 45–65 range depending on sugar concentration; (2) konjac glucomannan is a viscous soluble fibre documented to attenuate postprandial glucose response (GI-lowering effect), supporting the classification of GI 55 as 'low'; (3) Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2021) International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021, Am J Clin Nutr, used as the primary reference framework. GL per serving = (55 × 46.0 g available carbohydrate per 500 g serving) / 100 = 25.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~25 mg per 500 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Iced peach tea with konjac jelly contains negligible sodium (~25 mg per standard 500 ml serving), posing minimal direct risk for hypertension; however, individuals should be aware that added sugar contributes to overall caloric load and metabolic risk, and should request reduced-sugar options when available.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low-to-moderate glycemic index (~55) and significant sugar load (~39 g per 500 ml serving) from added peach syrup
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with this beverage due to its low-to-moderate GI of approximately 55 and an estimated 39 g of free sugars per standard 500 ml serving; requesting a lower-sugar formulation (e.g., 0–30% sugar level) and accounting for the glycemic load in daily carbohydrate budgeting is strongly advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and very low sodium; moderate free sugar content is the primary concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This beverage contains negligible saturated fat and very low sodium, which are favourable for cardiovascular health; however, the moderate free sugar content (~39 g per 500 ml serving) may contribute to hypertriglyceridaemia and excess caloric intake if consumed frequently, and reduced-sugar versions are preferable for individuals managing cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Iced Peach Tea with Konjac Jelly (蜜桃纤果冰茶) is a chilled, sweetened tea beverage popular at Taiwanese-style tea shops in Singapore, including the CHICHA San Chen chain. It is made by brewing black or green tea, sweetening it with peach-flavoured syrup or peach concentrate, chilling it over ice, and adding chewy konjac jelly pieces as a topping. Konjac jelly is made from glucomannan, a soluble dietary fibre derived from the konjac plant (Amorphophallus konjac), and contributes a translucent, firm-yet-yielding texture that contrasts pleasantly with the cold, lightly astringent tea. The beverage has a sweet, fruity peach aroma and flavour with mild tea bitterness and a refreshing icy character. Nutritionally, it is primarily a source of free sugars from the peach syrup, with modest carbohydrate content (~9.2 g per 100 g) and very low protein and fat. The konjac jelly component contributes a small amount of dietary fibre (~0.6 g per 100 g), which may modestly attenuate postprandial glucose response. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low), making it a low-to-moderate glycemic beverage. Sodium content is very low (~5 mg per 100 g). Clinically, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and sugar level selection, as a standard 500 ml serving at full sugar may contain approximately 39 g of free sugars. The beverage is dairy-free, gluten-free, and suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets. It is commonly sold at customisable sugar levels (0%, 30%, 50%, 70%, 100%) and ice levels, allowing consumers to reduce glycemic impact.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for iced peach tea with konjac jelly exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a composite product. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for brewed tea, sweetened fruit-flavoured tea, and konjac jelly, combined using a typical Taiwanese tea shop formulation. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database framework for sweetened tea beverages, adjusted for the glucomannan fibre contribution of konjac jelly. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level evidence is from approved primary sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero, as this is a tea-based beverage with no fat-containing ingredients; confirmed zero per USDA FDC brewed tea and sweetened tea entries. 2. saturated_fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero by product composition. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are 0.0: naturally zero for this beverage. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero; no hydrogenated fats present. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero; no animal-derived fat-containing ingredients. 6. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1: not reported in the USDA FDC source entries used for this estimation. 7. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1: not reported in the source entries used. 8. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1: not reported in the source entries used. 9. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1: not reported in the source entries used. 10. vitamin_b6_mg is -1: not reported in the source entries used. 11. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1: not reported; plant-based beverage with no B12 source. 12. folate_mcg_dfe is -1: not reported in the source entries used. 13. vitamin_d_mcg is -1: not reported; no vitamin D source in this beverage. 14. vitamin_e_mg is -1: not reported in the source entries used. 15. phosphorus_mg is -1: not reported in the source entries used. 16. magnesium_mg is -1: not reported in the source entries used. 17. zinc_mg is -1: not reported in the source entries used. 18. selenium_mcg is -1: not reported in the source entries used. 19. glycemic_index set to 55 (low) as an analogous estimate for a sweetened fruit-flavoured tea beverage; konjac glucomannan fibre is documented to attenuate postprandial glucose response, supporting the classification of GI 55 as 'low' per University of Sydney GI Database guidelines. 20. GL per serving calculated as (55 × 46.0 g available carbohydrate in 500 g serving) / 100 = 25.3. 21. Sugar level at standard full-sugar formulation assumed for nutrient estimation; reduced-sugar options are available and would substantially lower energy, sugar_g, and glycemic load. 22. Food is served by CHICHA San Chen at One Fullerton, Singapore (field survey data source). 23. protein_g is 0.1 g per 100 g, reflecting trace protein from tea solids and konjac; not zero.
